examples.title: Recursive Sum of First n Natural Numbers
examples.description: Algorithm to compute 1+2+3+...+n recursively with induction-based correctness proof
examples.code:
def sum_n(n):
    # Base Case
    if n == 1:
        return 1
    # Inductive Step
    return n + sum_n(n - 1)

# Proof by Induction:
# Base Case: sum_n(1) = 1 = 1(1+1)/2 ✓
# Inductive Hypothesis: sum_n(k) = k(k+1)/2
# Inductive Step: sum_n(k+1) = k+1 + sum_n(k)
#                        = k+1 + k(k+1)/2
#                        = (k+1)(1 + k/2)
#                        = (k+1)(k+2)/2 ✓
examples.explanation: The recursive algorithm mirrors the inductive structure. The base case handles n=1, and the recursive call assumes correctness for n-1 (inductive hypothesis), then adds n to prove correctness for n.

examples.title: Recursive Factorial with Induction Proof
examples.description: Factorial computation using recursion with correctness proof
examples.code:
def factorial(n):
    # Base Case
    if n == 0 or n == 1:
        return 1
    # Inductive Step
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

# Proof by Induction:
# Base Case: factorial(0) = factorial(1) = 1 = 0! = 1! ✓
# Inductive Hypothesis: factorial(k) = k!
# Inductive Step: factorial(k+1) = (k+1) × factorial(k)
#                              = (k+1) × k!
#                              = (k+1)! ✓
examples.explanation: Proves factorial(n) = n! for all n≥0. Each recursive call reduces the problem size by 1, building up the multiplication chain.

examples.title: Binary Search with Loop Invariant
examples.description: Binary search demonstrating loop invariant proof technique
examples.code:
def binary_search(arr, target):
    low, high = 0, len(arr) - 1
    
    # Loop Invariant: target, if present, is in arr[low:high+1]
    while low <= high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        
        # Maintenance: Check if target is in left or right half
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid
        elif arr[mid] < target:
            low = mid + 1  # Search right half
        else:
            high = mid - 1  # Search left half
        # Invariant preserved: target still in remaining range
    
    # Termination: low > high, target not found
    return -1

# Proof using Loop Invariant:
# 1. Initialization: low=0, high=n-1, target in original array
# 2. Maintenance: Each iteration eliminates half, keeps target in range
# 3. Termination: low > high, exhaustively searched, return -1 if not found
examples.explanation: The loop invariant proves correctness: the target, if it exists, must be within the search range [low, high] at all times. Each iteration maintains this property while shrinking the range.

examples.title: Fibonacci Sequence with Strong Induction
examples.description: Fibonacci computation demonstrating strong induction usage
examples.explanation: This algorithm naturally fits strong induction because the value at position k+1 depends on TWO previous values (k and k-1), not just one. This is why both base cases (n=1, n=2) are needed.
